FT_IC1.evidence: "Prospectively collected data from the ABCD study were analyzed from 3 time points: baseline (2016-2018, ages 9-10 years), year 1 (2017-2019), and year 2 (2018-2020)"

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using group-based trajectory modeling to characterize longitudinal patterns of social media use over time, with linear regression analyses estimating associations with cognitive performance

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed JAMA letter (December 2, 2025, Volume 334, Number 21)

FT_IC2.evidence: Social media time (hours per day) trajectories are IV; cognitive performance (NIH Toolbox subtests: ORRT, PSMT, PVT, composite score) is DV. Qualifying individual-trait-level outcome.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple linear regression analyses reported for each cognitive subtest and composite score with trajectory groups as predictors

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric results reported for all cognitive outcomes: ORRT difference -1.39 (95% CI, -2.10 to -0.68) for low increasing vs. no/very low; PSMT -2.03 (95% CI, -2.91 to -1.15); PVT -2.09 (95% CI, -2.78 to -1.41); composite -0.85 (95% CI, -1.30 to -0.40)

FT_EC7_reason: Longitudinal design but social media is IV (predictor) and cognitive performance is DV (outcome), not SM-as-DV-only
notes: Prospective design across 3 timepoints with ages 9-13 years; three trajectory groups identified; adjustment for baseline confounders including age, sex, race/ethnicity, household income, parent education, ADHD symptoms, depression, baseline cognitive score, other screen time, and study site

FT_IC1.evidence: "In this prospective cohort study using data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study across 21 study sites from October 2016 to October 2018, children aged 9 to 10 years at baseline were assessed across 4 waves"

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using random-intercept cross-lagged panel models (RI-CLPM) to examine reciprocal associations between social media use and depressive symptoms across 4 annual waves

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed JAMA Network Open article (May 21, 2025, 8(5):e2511704)

FT_IC2.evidence: Self-reported time spent on social media at baseline to 3-year follow-up (both IV and DV tested); depressive symptoms from Child Behavior Checklist (CBCL) depressive problems score (both IV and DV tested). Bidirectional within-person associations examined.

FT_IC3.evidence: Cross-lagged structural equation panel models with within-person and between-person associations tested across 4 measurement occasions

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple quantitative results reported: within-person year 1 to year 2 β=0.07 (95% CI, 0.01-0.12, P=.01); year 2 to year 3 β=0.09 (95% CI, 0.04-0.14, P<.001); between-person β=-0.01 (95% CI, -0.04 to 0.02, P=.46); autoregressive coefficients ranging β=0.17 to 0.35

FT_EC7_reason: Longitudinal design with bidirectional associations tested (SM both as IV and DV in analyses); EC7 does not apply when SM appears as IV anywhere in the paper
notes: Large prospective sample (N=11,876 at baseline); random-intercept cross-lagged panel model accounting for between-person differences; covariates included sex, race/ethnicity, household income, parental education; good model fit indices achieved (CFI 0.977, TLI 0.968, RMSEA 0.031)

FT_IC1.evidence: "We analyzed prospective cohort data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study (N=9,243; ages 10–11 years in Year 1 in 2017–2019)"

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical prospective analysis using linear regression to determine associations between typical daily screen time (Year 1) and manic symptoms (Year 3), with mediation analyses testing indirect effects

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Social Psychiatry and Psychiatric Epidemiology (2025, 60:1479–1487)

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time subtypes measured as IV (mean 0.2±0.6 h/d for social media; 1.2±1.2 h/d for video games; 0.4±0.7 h/d for texting; 1.2±1.2 h/d for videos); manic symptoms measured as DV (7 Up subscale). Social media, texting, videos, and video games all showed qualifying associations with manic symptoms.

FT_IC3.evidence: Linear regression analyses with robust standard errors; mediation models using generalized structural equation models; multiple associations tested across screen subtypes

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple quantitative results: overall screen time B=0.05 (95% CI 0.03, 0.07, p<0.001); social media B=0.20 (95% CI 0.09, 0.32, p=0.001); texting B=0.18 (95% CI 0.08, 0.28, p<0.001); videos B=0.14 (95% CI 0.08, 0.19, p<0.001); video games B=0.09 (95% CI 0.04, 0.14, p=0.001); indirect effects reported with bias-corrected 95% CIs

FT_EC7_reason: Longitudinal design (Year 1 to Year 3) with screen time/SM as IV and manic symptoms as DV (not SM-as-DV-only); EC7 does not apply
notes: Prospective design across 3 years; N=9,243 after exclusions for missing data; mediation analyses tested sleep duration and problematic SM/video game use as partial mediators (47.7%, 58.0%, and 9.0% mediation, respectively); adjusted for age, sex, race/ethnicity, household income, parent education, study site, baseline manic symptoms, ADHD, depressive symptoms, and COVID-19 timeframe
